theme: guess the recipe from the grocery bills
title: Nonna's Ledger — recovering the batch-prep op-count recurrence
strategies: trivial: emit T(n) ~= n (ignores the ledger's growth rate and structure entirely) -> reproduces the checker's calibrated floor, ~0.08-0.10

greedy: least-squares fit a linear (or linear + mod-5-averaged-residual) closed-form model of T(n) directly against the ledger; this captures the dominant linear trend almost perfectly on nearby sizes, but the true op-count is generated by a two-branch recursion whose rounding/parity corrections compound at every recursive level -- so absolute error grows like n^0.7-0.8 while the smooth model only ever improves the O(1) top-level slice of it; near-zero exact matches at mega-order sizes

strong: notice residuals R(n) = T(n) - T(floor(n/A)) - T(ceil(n/B)) collapse to a function of n alone once the right (A,B) is found; search the small divisor grid, solve for the linear coefficient C from two same-residue ledger rows, verify R(n) - C*n depends ONLY on n mod M across the WHOLE ledger (an exact integer identity, not an approximate fit), and emit the recovered two-branch recursive program -- reproduces the hidden law exactly at any extrapolated size

headroom above strong: a policy that also minimises the emitted program's footprint or handles pathological base-range edge cases even more robustly could close the remaining gap toward the scoring ceiling
